title: The Virgin Mary (Monthly) — St. Julian of Tarsus & St. Tertullius the Martyr
narrative:
The Monthly Commemoration of Our Most Holy Lady the Theotokos and Ever-Virgin Mary, St. Julian the Soldier-Martyr of Tarsus, and St. Tertullius the Martyr

On the twenty-first of Sene, the holy Church celebrates the monthly commemoration of our Most Holy Lady the Theotokos and Ever-Virgin Mary, the Mother of God, the Queen of Heaven and Earth, the one whose 'Yes' to the angel Gabriel set in motion the entire economy of salvation and whose maternal intercession the tradition regards as the most powerful and most constant of all creaturely intercession before the throne of God. The monthly commemoration of the Theotokos in the Ethiopian calendar is one of the most theologically rich recurring feasts in the entire liturgical year: the community that gathers monthly around the Virgin Mary's feast is a community that affirms monthly the foundational Christological truth that the Council of Ephesus defined — that she who bore the eternal Son of God in her womb is rightly called God-bearer, that the Incarnation was not a matter of divine power descending from a safe distance but of the eternal Word taking flesh from the flesh of a particular woman, making her body the first tabernacle of the New Covenant and her maternity the pattern of all subsequent receptivity to the divine word.

The theology of Mary in the Tewahedo tradition is a theology of the new creation: Mary is the second Eve in whom the first Eve's 'No' is reversed, the woman whose free assent to the divine word repairs the damage done by the first woman's assent to the serpent's word, and whose child is the second Adam in whom all that Adam lost is recovered and more than recovered. But the tradition does not rest content with Mary as merely the instrument of a theological transaction: she is honoured as a person in her own right, as the mother who pondered all these things in her heart, who stood at the Cross when others fled, who was present at Pentecost when the Spirit came on the community that would become the Church. The monthly return of her feast is the calendar's acknowledgment that her intercession is as perpetual as her motherhood, that the woman who once carried God in her womb continues to carry the needs of God's children before the throne of her Son.

Saint Julian of Tarsus is a martyr of the early period of persecution whose association with Tarsus — the city of Paul the Apostle — gives his feast a particularly resonant geography: to die for the faith in the city where Paul was born is, in some sense, to die in the shadow of the greatest of all the apostolic witnesses, in the city from which the first great missionary journey of the Church's history was set in motion by a man who had himself been converted on the road from Damascus. Julian's martyrdom in Tarsus is the tradition's testimony that the apostolic inheritance of a city is carried forward not only by the bishops and theologians who succeed the apostles in formal succession but by the ordinary faithful who die for the same faith that the apostle proclaimed, whose blood is the continuation of the apostolic seed in the soil that the apostle first prepared.

Saint Tertullius the Martyr completes the assembly of this twenty-first day of Sene as a witness whose faithful death for the confession of Christ is honoured by the tradition with the reverence given to every member of the great cloud of witnesses that the Letter to the Hebrews describes as surrounding the running community of faith. Tertullius's name is the name of a Roman lawyer — the same name as the advocate who prosecuted Paul before Felix in the Acts of the Apostles — and the tradition finds in this coincidence a quiet theological irony: where the Tertullus of the Acts sought Paul's death for the faith, this Tertullius died for it, the accuser's name transformed into the martyr's name, the hostile advocate become the faithful witness. The twenty-first day of Sene gathers around the monthly feast of the Queen of all the martyrs and apostles two witnesses who died in her Son's name in cities whose apostolic pedigree enriches the witness they gave. Amen.
